name: Mexican Drug War
summary: Ongoing conflict among Mexican security forces, drug cartels, cartel factions, local gangs, and self-defense groups that intensified after the federal militarized crackdown launched in December 2006. More than 463,000 homicides have been recorded in Mexico since 2006, but only a subset can be securely attributed to organized-crime or drug-war violence. Victim status is usually unclear; UCDP found that almost 97% of Mexico's 2024 organized-violence deaths were classified as unknown civilian/combatant status.

military_killed.notes: Minimum assembled from non-identical series: PGR reported 3,074 public servants killed in 2006-2011, including 2,851 agents; SEDENA data cited 610 soldiers killed from 1 Dec 2006 to 30 Apr 2022; Causa en Comun reported 2,456 police killed from 1 Dec 2018 to 30 Sep 2024, 348 police killed in 2025, and 11 in the first 15 days of 2026. This is not a complete official total and leaves gaps, especially 2012-2017 and post-2022 military deaths.

breakdown.victims_group: Civilians in Tlatlaya, State of Mexico
breakdown.notes: HRW and Mexican human-rights findings report 22 civilians killed by soldiers in June 2014; unlawful/extrajudicial status is disputed in litigation but the case is a core state-abuse case.
CNDH concluded Federal Police extrajudicially killed at least 22 of 42 men killed in the 2015 Tanhuato operation.
Investigative and IACHR-cited reporting alleged at least 16 civilians were extrajudicially executed by federal agents in January 2015.

breakdown.victims_group: Ayotzinapa students and civilians in Iguala
breakdown.notes: The Iguala/Ayotzinapa attack left 43 students disappeared and 6 people killed. The disappeared are not counted as confirmed deaths in the low figure, but a government truth panel later said evidence suggested the students were killed and disappeared.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: Only emblematic documented cases are included. Reports that the military killed about 3,000 people in 2007-2012 cannot be used as a civilian total because victims were often recorded as presumed aggressors and not reliably separated into cartel combatants, civilians, or unlawful executions.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Civilians indirectly harmed by militarization, disappearances, torture, displacement and impunity
deaths_from_actions.notes: No credible national series converts these mechanisms into attributable indirect deaths. The 130,178 persons still registered as disappeared from 2006-2026 are a mass-victimization indicator but cannot be counted as deaths.

military_killed.notes: No reliable all-period count of cartel combatants killed exists. UCDP records cartel-vs-cartel conflict fatalities, but Mexico's organized-violence deaths are overwhelmingly difficult to classify as civilian or combatant.

breakdown.notes: UCDP one-sided violence category for 2024; best estimate 53. This is a narrow category and excludes many cartel-related deaths of unknown status.

breakdown.victims_group: Civilians killed by Sinaloa Cartel in 2024
breakdown.notes: UCDP one-sided violence category for 2024; best estimate 43. This is a narrow category and excludes many cartel-related deaths of unknown status.

breakdown.victims_group: Victims of organized-crime-style homicides, mixed civilian/combatant/unknown status
breakdown.notes: Justice in Mexico found organized-crime-style homicides represented 25.4%-44.0% of INEGI homicides in 2016 depending on source, or 30.4%-52.8% of SNSP homicides. This cannot be used as a clean civilian-only count.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: The low is only UCDP's 2024 high estimates for CJNG plus Sinaloa one-sided civilian killings. The true 2006-2026 civilian toll from cartels is vastly higher but cannot be separated from combatant and unknown-status organized-crime deaths with confidence.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Disappeared persons, many likely killed but not confirmed dead
deaths_from_actions.notes: Government update reported 130,178 persons still disappeared from 2006-2026. Cartels and organized crime are widely identified as major perpetrators, but disappearances cannot be converted into deaths without case-level confirmation.
Cartel control, extortion, forced recruitment and displacement plausibly cause indirect mortality, but no credible nationwide death estimate was found.

military_killed.notes: No reliable national, conflict-wide series was found for self-defense combatants killed.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: Some self-defense groups committed abuses or were absorbed into cartel/state-aligned violence, but no credible national civilian-death estimate was found.

totals.notes: The military_low is a minimum for security/public officials killed. The civilian_low combines documented emblematic state-abuse cases and UCDP's narrow 2024 one-sided cartel civilian killings, so it is not a real all-period civilian toll. The grand_low is an analytic lower-bound estimate for organized-crime/drug-war-related deaths based on official early drug-war counts, organized-crime homicide shares, and later annual organized-crime homicide levels. The grand_high is the approximate all-homicide envelope since 2006 through early 2026, not all of which is drug-war-related.

key_atrocities.name: San Fernando massacre
key_atrocities.notes: 72 migrants murdered in Tamaulipas; reports identify 58 men and 14 women.

key_atrocities.name: Allende / northern Coahuila massacre
key_atrocities.victims_group: Families and civilians accused of links to rivals or informants
key_atrocities.notes: Narrow case files support dozens of victims; broader accounts and former Zetas testimony allege up to about 300 killed/disappeared across several municipalities.

key_atrocities.name: Casino Royale arson
key_atrocities.notes: Arson attack in Monterrey killed 52 people.

key_atrocities.name: Cadereyta massacre
key_atrocities.notes: 49 mutilated bodies found dumped near Cadereyta, Nuevo Leon; officials attributed the killings to Los Zetas.

key_atrocities.name: Iguala / Ayotzinapa disappearance
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Local police, Guerreros Unidos, and state-linked actors
key_atrocities.notes: 6 people killed and 43 students disappeared; a government truth panel later characterized the disappearance as a state-sponsored crime.

key_atrocities.name: Tlatlaya massacre
key_atrocities.notes: 22 civilians killed by soldiers in State of Mexico.

key_atrocities.name: Tanhuato killings
key_atrocities.notes: 42 men killed; CNDH concluded at least 22 were extrajudicially executed.

uncertainty_notes: The principal uncertainty is classification: homicide data are strong, but perpetrator and victim status are weak. Official organized-crime homicide series were discontinued; private/media series vary; UCDP reports that almost 97% of Mexico's organized-violence deaths in 2024 were unknown civilian/combatant status. Disappearances are a major hidden-mortality indicator but cannot be counted as deaths without confirmation.

sources.title: Mexico's Long War: Drugs, Crime, and the Cartels

The organised crime landscape in Mexico
